content.problemStatement:
Chef has a binary string SSS of length NNN. Chef can perform the following operation on SSS:


Insert any character (000 or 111) at any position in SSS.
Find the minimum number of operations Chef needs to perform so that no two consecutive characters are same in SSS.


Input Format

The first line contains a single integer TTT — the number of test cases. Then the test cases follow.
The first line of each test case contains an integer NNN — the length of the binary string SSS.
The second line of each test case contains a binary string SSS of length NNN containing 000s and 111s only.


Output Format
For each test case, output on a new line the minimum number of operations Chef needs to perform so that no two consecutive characters are same in SSS.
content.inputFormat: First line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each test case consists of two lines: the first line contains an integer N, the length of the binary string S. The second line contains the binary string S of length N consisting only of characters '0' and '1'.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, output a single integer on a new line — the minimum number of insert operations required so that no two consecutive characters in the resulting string are the same.

sampleTestCases.explanation: Test case 1: "11" has one equal adjacent pair → 1 insertion. Test case 2: "0101" already alternates → 0 insertions. Test case 3: "00100" has equal pairs at positions (1,2) and (4,5) → 2 insertions.
First case: single character needs no insertion. Second case: "110011" consists of three runs of length 2, each requiring 1 insertion → total 3.
The string already has no two consecutive equal characters.

solution.approach: Whenever two consecutive characters are equal (S[i]==S[i-1]) we must insert the opposite bit between them. One insertion fixes that pair and does not create a new equal adjacency. Hence the answer equals the number of positions i (1<=i<N) with S[i]==S[i-1]. Scan the string once and count such positions.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N) per test case, O(total length of all strings) overall.

code.C:
#include <stdio.h>
int main(){int T;if(scanf("%d",&T)!=1)return 0;while(T--){int N;scanf("%d",&N);char S[1000005];scanf("%s",S);int ans=0;for(int i=1;i<N;++i)if(S[i]==S[i-1])++ans;printf("%d\n",ans);}return 0;}
code.Java:
import java.io.*;
public class Main{public static void main(String[] args)throws Exception{BufferedReader br=new BufferedReader(new InputStreamReader(System.in));StringBuilder out=new StringBuilder();int T=Integer.parseInt(br.readLine().trim());while(T-- >0){int N=Integer.parseInt(br.readLine().trim());String S=br.readLine().trim();int ans=0;for(int i=1;i<N;++i)if(S.charAt(i)==S.charAt(i-1))++ans;out.append(ans).append('\n');}System.out.print(out.toString());}}
code.Python3:
import sys
def main():
    data=sys.stdin.read().split()
    if not data:
        return
    it=iter(data)
    T=int(next(it))
    out=[]
    for _ in range(T):
        N=int(next(it))
        S=next(it)
        ans=sum(1 for i in range(1,N) if S[i]==S[i-1])
        out.append(str(ans))
    sys.stdout.write('\n'.join(out))
if __name__=='__main__':
    main()
